```{r vmr_icc_neg}
  theme_minimal()

# show plot
print(plot)

# save plot
plot_name <- sprintf("%s_%d_icc_variance_decomposition_neg_1.png", prefix, plot_counter)
save_plot(plot, plot_name)
plot_counter <- plot_counter + 1
```

```{r vmr_icc_act_neg}
# Variables to analyze (VMR)
dyad_level_vars <- c("PA_similarity_act", "NA_similarity_act")

# Simplified 2-level loop (Segments < Dyad)
icc_table_dyad_only_neg <- dyad_level_vars %>%
  map_dfr(function(v) {
    formula_str <- as.formula(paste0(v, " ~ 1 + (1 | dyad)"))
    model <- lmer(formula_str, data = vmr_data_neg)
    icc_data <- icc(model) # Standard ICC for 2-level
    data.frame(
      Variable = v,
      ICC_Dyad = icc_data$ICC_adjusted,
      ICC_Person = "-" # not applicable in 2-level model
    )
  })

print(icc_table_dyad_only_neg)

plot_data <- icc_table_dyad_only_neg %>%
  mutate(Momentary = 1 - (ICC_Dyad)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(ICC_Dyad, Momentary),
    names_to = "Level",
    values_to = "Proportion"
  )

plot <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Variable, y = Proportion, fill = Level)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_discrete(type = hcl.colors(4, palette = "Grays", alpha = 0.8)) +
  labs(title = "VMR Negative - Variance Decomposition by Variable",
       x = "", y = "Percentage of Total Variance") +
  theme_minimal()

print(plot)

plot_name <- sprintf("%s_%d_vmr_icc_variance_decomposition_neg_2.png", prefix, plot_counter)
save_plot(plot, plot_name)
plot_counter <- plot_counter + 1

```

### Comparison

```{r VMR_icc_comparison}
# 1) Bind + reshape to wide with a stable naming scheme: <Interaction>__<Metric>
icc_table_wide <- bind_rows(
  "All"      = icc_table_all,
  "Pos" = icc_table_pos,
  "Neg" = icc_table_neg,
  .id = "Interaction"
) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(ICC_Dyad, ICC_Person, ICC_Moment),
    names_to  = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from  = c(Interaction, Metric),
    values_from = Value,
    names_sep   = "_"
  ) %>%
  select(
    Variable,
    All_ICC_Dyad,      All_ICC_Person,      All_ICC_Moment,
    Pos_ICC_Dyad, Pos_ICC_Person, Pos_ICC_Moment,
    Neg_ICC_Dyad, Neg_ICC_Person, Neg_ICC_Moment
  )

# 2) gt table with spanners + repeated subheaders + vertical separators
icc_table_wide %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "All interactions",
    columns = c(All_ICC_Dyad, All_ICC_Person, All_ICC_Moment)
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Positive interaction",
    columns = c(Pos_ICC_Dyad, Pos_ICC_Person, Pos_ICC_Moment)
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Negative interaction",
    columns = c(Neg_ICC_Dyad, Neg_ICC_Person, Neg_ICC_Moment)
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    All_ICC_Dyad = "Dyad", All_ICC_Person = "Person", All_ICC_Moment = "Moment",
    Pos_ICC_Dyad = "Dyad", Pos_ICC_Person = "Person", Pos_ICC_Moment = "Moment",
    Neg_ICC_Dyad = "Dyad", Neg_ICC_Person = "Person", Neg_ICC_Moment = "Moment"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = -Variable,
    decimals = 2
  ) %>%
  cols_align("left",   columns = Variable) %>%
  cols_align("center", columns = -Variable) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(12),
    table.background.color = "white",
    heading.background.color = "white",
    column_labels.background.color = "white",
    row_group.background.color = "white"
  )
```

Some observations:

-   Within positive interactions, perceived NA similarity fluctuates less moment-to-moment and is more attributable to stable person/dyad differences. In a positive interaction, noticing negative cues may be more person/couple-specific, hence more between-person/dyad. Also perceived PA similarity becomes more dyad/person-linked in negative interactions than in positive interactions.

    Note: not analysed, but floor effects in NA during positive interactions can cause this I think.

-   Dyad-level variance is much larger in negative interactions for NA_own: .10 (All) → .04 (Positive) → .26 (Negative). This suggests couples differ much more in their overall NA during negative interactions.

-   The perceived partner affects show more stable components in the valenced subsets. So perceptions of the partner are less “moment-only” once you condition on interaction valence.

## Similarity indices

### Theoretical similarity surface

The similarity indices are constructed in the same way as for the ESM data, so the `base_surface` is the same.

### Similarity in the sample

#### Ranges and distribution

```{r VMR_similarity_pracical_ranges}

sim_cols <- c("PA_similarity_act", "NA_similarity_act", 
              "PA_similarity_perc", "NA_similarity_perc")

draw_violin_plot <- function() {
  vioplot(vmr_data[, sim_cols],
        main = "Range and Distribution of Affect Similarity",
        col = c("#A6CEE3", "#FB9A99", "#A6CEE3", "#FB9A99"),
        names = c("PA\nActual", "NA\nActual", "PA\nPerceived", "NA\nPerceived"),
        areaEqual = FALSE,   # FALSE = width is proportional to data density
        rectCol = "gray30",  # Color of the inner boxplot
        lineCol = "gray30",  # Color of the median line
        border = "gray30"    # Color of the violin border
)
title(ylab = "Similarity (S = M - D)", line = 2)
abline(h = 0, col = "black", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5) # Zero line
}

draw_violin_plot()

plot_name <- sprintf("%s_%d_violin.png", prefix, plot_counter)
save_base_plot(draw_violin_plot(), plot_name)
plot_counter <- plot_counter + 1

# numerical ranges
cat("Ranges (Min/Max):\n")
print(sapply(vmr_data[, sim_cols], range, na.rm = TRUE))
```

#### Actual PA similarity

```{r VMR_PA_act_obs_vs_theo}
#| warning: false

base_surface %>%
  add_layer(
    data = vmr_data,
    x = ~cPA_own,
    y = ~cPA_part_act,    
    z = ~PA_similarity_act,     
    title = "PA Actual Similarity: Observed vs. Theoretical",
    x_lab = "Actor PA (Cent.)",
    y_lab = "Partner PA (Cent.)",
    z_lab = "Actual PA Similarity Index"
  )
```

#### Actual NA similarity

```{r VMR_NA_act_obs_vs_theo}
#| warning: false

base_surface %>%
  add_layer(
    data = vmr_data,
    x = ~cNA_own,
    y = ~cNA_part_act,    
    z = ~NA_similarity_act,   
    title = "NA Actual Similarity: Observed vs. Theoretical",
    x_lab = "Actor NA (Cent.)",
    y_lab = "Partner NA (Cent.)",
    z_lab = "Actual NA Similarity Index"
  )
```

#### Perceived PA similarity

```{r VMR_PA_perc_obs_vs_theo}
#| warning: false

base_surface %>%
  add_layer(
    data = vmr_data,
    x = ~cPA_own,
    y = ~cPA_part_perc,    
    z = ~PA_similarity_perc,   
    title = "PA Perceived Similarity: Observed vs. Theoretical",
    x_lab = "Actor PA (Cent.)",
    y_lab = "Perceived Partner PA (Cent.)",
    z_lab = "Perceived PA Similarity Index"
  )
```

#### Perceived NA similarity

```{r VMR_NA_perc_obs_vs_theo}
#| warning: false

base_surface %>%
  add_layer(
    data = vmr_data,
    x = ~cNA_own,
    y = ~cNA_part_perc,    
    z = ~NA_similarity_perc,   
    title = "NA Perceived Similarity: Observed vs. Theoretical",
    x_lab = "Actor NA (Cent.)",
    y_lab = "Perceived Partner NA (Cent.)",
    z_lab = "Perceived NA Similarity Index"
  )
```

## Autocorrelation analysis

```{r vmr_autocorrelation}
#| warning: false

target_vars <- c("cPA_own", "cNA_own", "cPA_part_act", "cNA_part_act", 
                 "cPA_part_perc", "cNA_part_perc")

# calculate Lag-1 ac per person
# We group by dyad/person to ensure we don't lag across different people
acf_results <- vmr_data %>%
  group_by(dyad, person, topic) %>% # also group by topic here
  arrange(dyad, person, topic, segment) %>% # order by topic and time!
  summarise(
    across(all_of(target_vars), 
          ~ cor(., dplyr::lag(.), use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
           .names = "{.col}"
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# reshape for easy checking/plotting
acf_long <- acf_results %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(target_vars), 
               names_to = "Variable", 
               values_to = "Autocorrelation")

# summary
cat("--- Average Lag-1 Autocorrelation (Temporal Dependence) ---\n")
acf_summary <- acf_long %>%
  group_by(topic, Variable) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_ACF = mean(Autocorrelation, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_ACF = median(Autocorrelation, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_ACF = sd(Autocorrelation, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
print(acf_summary)

# Plot
ggplot(acf_long, aes(x = Variable, y = Autocorrelation, fill = Variable)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~topic) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Lag-1 Autocorrelation per Person",
    subtitle = "Faceted by interaction type",
    y = "Lag-1 Correlation (r)",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

```

The mean lag-1 autocorrelations in the VMR data are lower than in the ESM data and have large between-person variability (SDs). Because each person only has T=16 segments (per interaction topic), each correlation r is estimated from only n=T-1=15 adjacent pairs (or fewer when missingness is present). This can lead to

-   substantial downward bias in estimates of the correlations, known as the Hurwicz or Kendall bias (see Table 4 in @solanasLagoneAutocorrelationShort2010)

-   substantial sampling error. Using Fisher's z-transformation, the transformed correlation is approximately normal with $SE(z)≈1/\sqrt{(n−3)}$​, see @fisherProbableErrorCoefficient1921. Via back-transormation this implies $SE(r)≈(1−r^2)/\sqrt{n−3}$​. However, this SE is a rough heuristic and can even be too small since it assumes independent pairs, while the data are serially dependent.

The lower lag-1 correlations and higher SDs in VMR may therefore come from these bias and noise effects due to short series length in addition to potentially faster, more reactive affect dynamics at the 30 seconds timescale.

```{r VMR unload}
#| include: false
clean_config("VMR")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# POST

```{r POST_setup}
# load data
post_data  <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data_ana, "post_ana.rds"))
# load data configuration file 
load_config("POST")
# plot intialisation
plot_counter <- 1
prefix <- "POST"
```

### Relation between closeness and love

```{r data_prep_1}
# from wide to long format, with two rows per person, one for pos, one for neg
post_data_long <- post_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(love_neg, love_pos, close_neg, close_pos),
    names_to = c(".value", "context"), # Splitting names into variable + context
    names_sep = "_"                    # Separator is the underscore
  )

data_neg <- post_data_long %>% filter(context == "neg")
data_pos <- post_data_long %>% filter(context == "pos")
```

```{r pos_neg_pooled}
#| warning: false

# Q: "Is Love associated with Closeness across all contexts?"

# rmcorr
rmcorr(person, love, close, dataset = post_data_long)

# multilevel model
model_overall <- lmer(close ~ love + (1 | dyad/person), data = post_data_long)
summary(model_overall)
```

Random intercepts absorb person and dyad baselines (stable dyad and person differences) such that fixed effects are more reliable. Random effects absorb the stable, baseline differences between dyads (e.g., Dyad A is always closer than Dyad B). This prevents these baseline differences from skewing the results and ensures the Fixed Effect (the relationship between Love and Closeness) is estimated with the correct level of certainty (standard error), avoiding false positives due to non-independence.

Strong, positive, and statistically significant association between the love felt after pooled interaction and the closeness felt. Slope = .69, p \< .001.

```{r neg}
model_neg <- lmer(close ~ love + (1 | dyad), data = data_neg)
summary(model_neg)

# data_neg_scaled <- data_neg %>% 
#   mutate(love = scale(love), close = scale(close))

# r_neg_model <- lmer(close ~ love + (1 | dyad), data = data_neg_scaled)
# summary(r_neg_model) # The estimate for 'love' is your correlation r.
```

Strong, positive, and statistically significant association between the love felt after a negative interaction and the closeness felt after that same interaction. Slope = .66, p \< .001. Dyad variance = 75.31 and residual variance = 154.59. ICC = 75.31/(75.31 + 154.59) = 0.33. About 33% of variation in closeness is due to the dyad the person belongs to.

```{r pos}
model_pos <- lmer(close ~ love + (1 | dyad), data = data_pos)
summary(model_pos)
```

Even stronger association with slope of .81 (p \< .001).

Remarkable: the variance for the random dyad intercept is 0. This should be interpreted carefully but suggests that the model found no stable differences between couples in how much closeness people feel. So, in the context of positive interactions, knowing which couple someone belongs to provides no additional information about their closeness **beyond what is already explained by their love**. After the negative scenario, couples had different baselines (variance = 75.31). **Some couples stayed closer than others even when love was low after the negative interaction.**

The association between love and closeness was significantly stronger following positive interactions (β=0.81) compared to negative interactions (β=0.66). This suggests that while love acts as a buffer during conflict—maintaining connection despite the friction of the argument—it acts as an enhancer during positive moments, where the absence of defensive barriers allows feelings of affection to translate more directly into feelings of intimacy.

```{r POST unload}
#| include: false
clean_config("POST")
```
